quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 6 Quiz 1
quiz.subtitle: Describing Waves
quiz.subject: Physics
quiz.topic: Grade 10 - Waves and Simple Harmonic Motion

questions.question: In a transverse wave, the particles of the medium vibrate:
options: Parallel to the direction of wave travel
Perpendicular to the direction of wave travel
In circular paths, transferring energy from one point to another
Only at the surface of the medium
questions.explanation: In a transverse wave, particle vibration is perpendicular to the direction the wave travels. Examples include waves on a string and light waves.

questions.question: In a longitudinal wave, the particles of the medium vibrate:
options: Perpendicular to the direction of wave travel
Parallel to the direction of wave travel
In elliptical paths, with permanent displacement of the medium
Only at the surface of the medium
questions.explanation: In a longitudinal wave, particles vibrate back and forth along the same direction the wave travels. Sound waves are longitudinal.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a transverse wave?
questions.explanation: Waves on a guitar string are transverse — the string vibrates up and down while the wave travels along the string.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a longitudinal wave?
questions.explanation: Sound waves in air are longitudinal — air particles vibrate back and forth along the direction the sound travels.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly classifies waves?
options: Sound waves are electromagnetic, light waves are mechanical
Sound waves are mechanical, light waves are electromagnetic
Both sound and light waves are mechanical
Both sound and light waves are electromagnetic
questions.explanation: Sound waves are mechanical (require a medium). Light waves are electromagnetic (can travel through a vacuum).

questions.question: The highest point of a transverse wave is called the:
questions.explanation: The crest is the highest point of a transverse wave. The lowest point is called the trough.

questions.question: The amplitude of a wave is the:
options: Distance between two consecutive crests or troughs
Number of wave cycles in one second
Maximum displacement from the equilibrium position
The total time taken for a wave to complete one oscillation
questions.explanation: Amplitude is the maximum distance a particle moves from its rest (equilibrium) position.

questions.question: The wavelength of a wave may be defines as the:
options: Maximum displacement from the equilibrium point
Number of waves passing a point per second
Total time taken for one complete oscillation
questions.explanation: Wavelength ($\lambda$) is the distance between two successive crests or two successive troughs.

questions.question: The relationship between wave speed ($v$), frequency ($f$), and wavelength ($\lambda$) is:
questions.explanation: The wave equation is $v = f\lambda$. Speed equals frequency multiplied by wavelength.

questions.question: A wave has a frequency of $50\text{ Hz}$ and a wavelength of $6.0\text{ m}$. What is its speed?
Which type of wave requires a medium to travel?
questions.explanation: Mechanical waves (sound, water, seismic) require a medium. Electromagnetic waves (light, radio) can travel through a vacuum.

questions.question: In a longitudinal wave, regions where particles are closest together are called:
questions.explanation: Compressions are regions of high pressure where particles are bunched together. Rarefactions are regions where particles are spread apart.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the fundamentals of wave types and properties!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the difference between transverse and longitudinal waves.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on wave vocabulary: crest, trough, amplitude, wavelength, compression, rarefaction.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: types of waves, parts of a wave, and the wave equation $v = f\lambda$.
